summary: The 33rd U.S. President, a "failed haberdasher" from Missouri who rose through the Pendergast political machine and succeeded to the presidency on the death of Franklin Roosevelt. Clues most often identify him through his decision to drop the atomic bombs on Hiroshima and Nagasaki, his 1948 upset election over Thomas Dewey (memorialized by the "Dewey Defeats Truman" headline photo), his sparring with the "Do-Nothing" 80th Congress, his Cold War foreign policy (the Truman Doctrine, NATO, recognition of Israel), and a 1950 assassination attempt by Puerto Rican nationalists at Blair House.

works.name: General / Giveaway Identifiers
works.description: Giveaway clues most commonly identify Truman as the "failed haberdasher" who became president upon Franklin Roosevelt's death, the man who authorized dropping the atomic bombs on Japan, the 33rd president, and the underdog who defeated Thomas E. Dewey in the 1948 election.

clues.clue: Authorized/ordered the dropping of the atomic bombs on Hiroshima and Nagasaki
examples: name this president who authorized the use of the atomic bomb on Japan.
name this president who authorized dropping the atomic bomb on Japan.

clues.clue: A "failed haberdasher" who became president following the death of Franklin Roosevelt
examples: name this failed haberdasher who became President following the death of Franklin Roosevelt.

clues.clue: Recognized the state of Israel in 1948
examples: name this US president who recognized the state of Israel in 1948.

clues.clue: Surprisingly defeated Thomas E. Dewey in the 1948 presidential election
examples: name this president who defeated Republican Thomas E. Dewey in 1948.
name this president who surprisingly defeated Dewey in the 1948 presidential election and chose not to contest the 1952 presidential election won by Eisenhower.

clues.clue: The 33rd President of the United States
examples: name this event which threatened the life of the 33rd President.

works.name: Rise to the Presidency
works.description: Truman's political career began under the patronage of the corrupt Pendergast political machine in Missouri, which helped elect him to the Senate and earned him the derisive nickname "the Senator from Pendergast." In the Senate, he chaired a namesake committee investigating fraud and waste in WWII military contracts, was renominated in a contested 1940 primary, and was picked as FDR's running mate in 1944 before succeeding to the presidency on Roosevelt's death in 1945.

clues.clue: Was first elected to the Senate with the help of the Pendergast political machine, earning him the nickname "the Senator from Pendergast"
examples: This president was first elected with the help of the Pendergast machine.
This politician, who vetoed the McCarran Act, was known as the 'Senator from Pendergast' while in Congress.

clues.clue: In the contested 1940 Senate primary, was supported by Robert Hannegan after former Governor Lloyd Stark challenged him for his seat
examples: In the 1940 election, this politician was supported by Robert Hannegan after former Governor Lloyd Stark challenged him for his seat in the primaries.

clues.clue: Led a namesake Senate committee that exposed fraud and waste in military contracts awarded during World War II
examples: He led a namesake Senate committee that exposed fraud and waste in military contracts awarded during the Second World War.

works.name: The Atomic Bomb and Its Aftermath
works.description: Truman's decision to drop the atomic bombs on Hiroshima and Nagasaki, made to end World War II, became a source of lasting ethical controversy. The philosopher G.E.M. Anscombe's essay "Mr. Truman's Degree" protested Oxford University's decision to award him an honorary degree, arguing his decision amounted to murder of the innocent.

clues.clue: The philosopher G.E.M. Anscombe's essay "Mr. Truman's Degree" protested Oxford University awarding him an honorary degree, arguing his bombing decision amounted to murder
examples: Name this person who titles an essay that contends 'Choosing to kill the innocent as a means to your ends is always murder.' That essay about this person objects to him receiving an honorary degree.
Name this man who was called 'murderous' in a speech opposing this man's reception of an honorary degree by the Oxford Encaenia.

works.name: Cold War Foreign Policy
works.description: Truman laid out the Truman Doctrine in a 1947 speech to Congress pledging support for anticommunist forces worldwide, a move often considered the formal declaration of the Cold War and a step toward the founding of NATO. His recognition of the state of Israel in 1948 was influenced by his former business partner Eddie Jacobson. His administration also intervened across the Middle East, ending an Iran Crisis by pressuring Soviet withdrawal from a separatist Azerbaijani government, backing a coup against Syrian president Shukri al-Quwatli to enable an oil pipeline, offering Saudi Arabia a "Golden Gimmick" tax credit splitting oil revenues 50/50, and repudiating FDR's promise of support to the Saudi king.

clues.clue: Laid out the Truman Doctrine in a 1947 speech to Congress, pledging support for anticommunist forces worldwide and often considered the formal declaration of the Cold War, leading toward NATO's founding
examples: This president's eponymous 'Doctrine' expressed support for anticommunist forces across the world and led to the foundation of NATO.
The namesake 'Doctrine' of this US president which was laid out in a 1947 speech to Congress is often considered to be the declaration of the Cold War.

clues.clue: Recognized the state of Israel in 1948, a decision influenced by his former business partner Eddie Jacobson
examples: This president's longtime friend Eddie Jacobson influenced an action he took against the advice of Dean Acheson and Secretary of State George Marshall.
This president's former business partner, Eddie Jacobson, influenced his decision to recognize the state of Israel.

clues.clue: Ended an Iran Crisis by pressuring the Soviets to withdraw support from a separatist Azerbaijani government
examples: This president ended an Iran Crisis by pressuring the Soviets to withdraw their support from a separatist Azerbaijani government.

clues.clue: Repudiated his predecessor's (FDR's) promise of support to the Saudi king, citing a relative lack of Arab constituents
examples: This president repudiated his predecessor's promise of support to the Saudi king by citing his relative lack of Arab constituents.

clues.clue: Gave Saudi Arabia a foreign tax credit called the "Golden Gimmick," splitting oil revenues 50/50 with American companies
examples: This president gave a foreign tax credit called the Golden Gimmick to Saudi Arabia, which split oil revenues 50/50 with American companies.

clues.clue: Supported a coup against Syrian president Shukri al-Quwatli that enabled the construction of an oil pipeline
examples: Under this president, the US supported a coup against Syrian president Shukri al-Quwatli that enabled the construction of an oil pipeline.

works.name: Domestic Policy and the "Do-Nothing" Congress
works.description: Truman repeatedly clashed with the Republican-controlled 80th Congress, which he dubbed the "Do-Nothing" Congress; it overrode his vetoes of the Taft-Hartley Act and the McCarran Internal Security Act (which established the Subversive Activities Control Board). Inflation spiked after Congress abolished the Office of Price Administration over his veto. He desegregated the U.S. armed forces, and his attempt to nationalize the steel industry during a labor dispute was struck down as unconstitutional in Youngstown Sheet & Tube Co. v. Sawyer. His Defense Secretary Louis A. Johnson unsuccessfully tried to merge the Marine Corps into the Army, and at least 20 people died in a smog event in Donora, Pennsylvania, during his presidency.

clues.clue: Congress overrode his veto of the Taft-Hartley Act (the Labor-Management Relations Act)
examples: Congress overrode this President's veto of legislation such as the McCarran Internal Security Act and the Taft-Hartley Act.
Congress overrode Harry Truman's veto of the Labor-Management Relations Act, co-sponsored by Fred Hartley and this Ohio senator.

clues.clue: Congress overrode his veto of the McCarran Internal Security Act, which established the Subversive Activities Control Board
examples: Congress overrode this president's veto on legislation that established the Subversive Activities Control Board.
This politician, who vetoed the McCarran Act, was known as the 'Senator from Pendergast' while in Congress.

clues.clue: Inflation spiked after Congress abolished the Office of Price Administration over his veto
examples: Inflation spiked after Congress abolished the Office of Price Administration over this president's veto; this man would later run against the 'Do Nothing' Congress.

clues.clue: His attempt to nationalize the steel industry during a labor dispute was struck down as unconstitutional in Youngstown Sheet & Tube Co. v. Sawyer
examples: This president's attempt to nationalize the steel industry was rejected in the Youngstown Sheet & Tube case.
Identify this president who frequently struggled with the Eightieth or 'Do Nothing' Congress. One of his actions was the subject of the Youngstown Sheet & Tube Co. case.

clues.clue: Desegregated the U.S. armed forces
examples: The Congress got its nickname for being notoriously uncooperative with this president, who desegregated the armed forces and succeeded FDR.

clues.clue: His Defense Secretary Louis A. Johnson unsuccessfully tried to merge the Marine Corps into the Army
examples: Under this president, Louis A. Johnson unsuccessfully tried to merge the Marine Corps into the Army.

clues.clue: At least 20 people were killed when a devastating smog blanketed the Pittsburgh suburb of Donora during his presidency
examples: During this man's presidency, at least 20 people were killed when a devastating smog blanketed the Pittsburgh suburb of Donora.

works.name: Scandals and Controversies
works.description: Truman's administration was dogged by corruption scandals: he fired Attorney General J. Howard McGrath after McGrath fired a special prosecutor investigating corruption in the Internal Revenue Bureau for being too zealous, and he helped save corrupt New York City mayor William O'Dwyer from prosecution by appointing him Ambassador to Mexico, though O'Dwyer was later recalled to testify before the Kefauver Committee on organized crime.

clues.clue: Fired Attorney General J. Howard McGrath after McGrath fired a special prosecutor investigating corruption in the Internal Revenue Bureau for being too zealous
examples: This president fired Attorney General J. Howard McGrath after McGrath fired a special prosecutor investigating corruption in the Internal Revenue Bureau for being too zealous.

clues.clue: Helped save corrupt New York City mayor William O'Dwyer from prosecution by appointing him Ambassador to Mexico; O'Dwyer was later recalled to testify before the Kefauver Committee on organized crime
examples: This president helped save corrupt New York City mayor William O'Dwyer from prosecution by appointing him Ambassador to Mexico; O'Dwyer was later recalled to testify to the Kefauver Committee on organized crime convened during this man's presidency.

works.name: The 1948 Election
works.description: Truman's 1948 re-election over Thomas E. Dewey is best remembered for a Chicago (Daily) Tribune edition that erroneously printed the headline "Dewey Defeats Truman," which Truman was famously photographed holding up and grinning about; the mistake stemmed partly from the paper's early printing deadline and partly from pollster Gallup's flawed landline-only sampling, which undercounted Truman's support (the psychic horse "Lady Wonder" reportedly made the same wrong prediction). During the campaign, his team financed the first-ever comic book biography of a presidential candidate, and Truman ran hard against the Republican-controlled "Do-Nothing" 80th Congress.

clues.clue: A Chicago (Daily) Tribune edition erroneously printed the headline "Dewey Defeats Truman"; Truman was later photographed cheerfully holding up the incorrect paper
examples: A Chicago Tribune headline incorrectly claimed that this president was defeated by Thomas Dewey in 1948.
In 1948, after winning an upset election, this incumbent president was photographed cheerfully holding a Chicago Tribune edition that claimed he had lost the election to Thomas Dewey.

clues.clue: Pollster Gallup undercounted his support partly because it sampled only households with a landline phone
examples: In one such mishap, Gallup only sampled households with a landline phone and thus underestimated this President, who defeated Thomas Dewey to win re-election in 1948.

clues.clue: The supposedly psychic horse "Lady Wonder," who claimed to predict every presidential election by ESP, also incorrectly predicted Dewey would defeat him
examples: Another supposedly-intelligent horse, Lady Wonder, allegedly used ESP to predict every presidential election - except that she, like the Daily Tribune, predicted that Dewey would defeat this man.

clues.clue: Ran his 1948 campaign against the "Do-Nothing" 80th Congress
examples: This president criticized the 'Do-Nothing' Congress, and he is depicted in a photograph holding up an incorrect headline by the Chicago Tribune, which claimed that Thomas Dewey had defeated him.
This president dealt with the 'Do-Nothing' Congress while campaigning in 1948 against Thomas Dewey.

clues.clue: His 1948 campaign financed the first-ever comic book biography of a presidential candidate, depicting "big business" men as cigar-chomping villains
examples: Commercial Comic's Malcolm Ater convinced this man's campaign to finance the first-ever comic book biography of a presidential candidate, with the villains being cigar-chomping 'big business' men.

works.name: Attempted Assassination at Blair House
works.description: On November 1, 1950, while Truman was staying at Blair House during White House renovations, Puerto Rican nationalists Oscar Collazo and Griselio Torresola attempted to assassinate him to draw attention to the Puerto Rican independence movement. White House policeman Leslie Coffelt was killed shooting Torresola; Collazo was wounded and later had his death sentence commuted. The attackers were inspired by the failed revolts of nationalist leader Pedro Albizu Campos, who was arrested after a shootout following the attempt.

clues.clue: On November 1, 1950, Puerto Rican nationalists Oscar Collazo and Griselio Torresola attempted to assassinate him outside Blair House
examples: This action took place on November 1, 1950 outside Blair House and was perpetrated by Oscar Collazo and Griselio Torresola to bring attention to the Puerto Rican nationalist movement.
While staying at Blair House, this man was the subject of an assassination attempt by two Puerto Rican nationalists.

clues.clue: White House policeman Leslie Coffelt was killed in the attack, and the wounded Collazo later had his death sentence commuted
examples: One participant in this event was shot in the head by Leslie Coffelt, and another was shot in the chest and later had his death sentence commuted.

clues.clue: The attackers were inspired by the failed revolts of Pedro Albizu Campos, who was arrested after a shootout in reaction to the attempt
examples: This action's perpetrators were inspired by the failed revolts of Pedro Albizu Campos, who was arrested after a shootout in reaction to this event.

clues.clue: Afterward remarked that the attackers were "as stupid as they could be" and joked he could have organized a better attempt himself
examples: The target of this event said its perpetrators were 'as stupid as they could be' and that he could have 'organize[d] a better program than the one they put on'.

works.name: Personal Life and Legacy
works.description: Famously plain-spoken, Truman once threatened to "beat the crap out of" music critic Paul Hume after Hume panned a concert by his daughter Margaret, calling him an "eight ulcer man on four ulcer pay," and joked that "a statesman is a politician who's been dead ten or fifteen years." The White House's second-floor balcony overlooking the South Lawn is named for him. After leaving office, he denounced his successor Dwight Eisenhower for failing to denounce Joseph McCarthy and endorsed Adlai Stevenson to succeed him; the Former Presidents Act was passed primarily to benefit him. His namesakes include the mascot of the University of Missouri.

clues.clue: Threatened to "beat the crap out of" music critic Paul Hume, calling him an "eight ulcer man on four ulcer pay," after Hume panned a concert by his daughter Margaret
examples: This politician called music critic Paul Hume an 'eight ulcer man on four ulcer pay' and threatened to beat the crap out of him after Hume published a negative review of a concert by his daughter.
This man joked that 'a statesman is a politician who's been dead ten or fifteen years,' and once hilariously threatened to beat the shit out of Paul Hume for insulting this man's daughter.

clues.clue: The White House's second-floor balcony overlooking the South Lawn is named for him (the Truman Balcony)
examples: The second-floor balcony overlooking the South Lawn of the White House is named for this politician.

clues.clue: After leaving office, denounced Dwight Eisenhower for failing to denounce Joseph McCarthy, and endorsed Adlai Stevenson to succeed him
examples: He denounced his successor for failing to denounce McCarthy and he endorsed Adlai E. Stevenson to succeed him.

clues.clue: The Former Presidents Act was passed primarily to benefit him
examples: The Former Presidents Act was primarily passed to benefit this politician, who was the target of an assassination attempt made at Blair House.

clues.clue: His namesakes include the mascot of the University of Missouri (Truman the Tiger) and his own Truman Doctrine
examples: This president's namesakes include the mascot of the University of Missouri and a doctrine opposing the spread of communism in Europe.

comprehensive_summary: Harry S. Truman, the 33rd U.S. President, was a "failed haberdasher" from Missouri whose political career began under the patronage of the corrupt Pendergast political machine, which helped elect him to the Senate and earned him the derisive nickname "the Senator from Pendergast." In the Senate he chaired a namesake committee investigating fraud and waste in World War II military contracts, survived a contested 1940 primary against Governor Lloyd Stark with the backing of Robert Hannegan, and was chosen as Franklin Roosevelt's running mate in 1944, succeeding to the presidency on Roosevelt's death in 1945. As president, Truman authorized the atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki to end World War II, a decision the philosopher G.E.M. Anscombe protested in her essay "Mr. Truman's Degree" after Oxford University awarded him an honorary degree. His foreign policy defined the early Cold War: the 1947 Truman Doctrine pledged support for anticommunist forces worldwide and helped lead to NATO's founding, he recognized the state of Israel in 1948 at the urging of his former business partner Eddie Jacobson, and his administration intervened repeatedly in the Middle East, ending an Iran Crisis by pressuring Soviet withdrawal from Azerbaijan, backing a coup against Syrian president Shukri al-Quwatli, offering Saudi Arabia a "Golden Gimmick" oil tax credit, and repudiating FDR's promises to the Saudi king. Domestically, he sparred constantly with the Republican-controlled "Do-Nothing" 80th Congress, which overrode his vetoes of the Taft-Hartley Act and the McCarran Internal Security Act; he desegregated the armed forces, saw his attempted nationalization of the steel industry struck down in Youngstown Sheet & Tube Co. v. Sawyer, and presided over a deadly Donora, Pennsylvania smog and a failed attempt by Defense Secretary Louis A. Johnson to merge the Marine Corps into the Army. His administration was also dogged by scandal, including the firing of Attorney General J. Howard McGrath over a fired IRS special prosecutor and his shielding of corrupt New York mayor William O'Dwyer with an ambassadorship. Truman's 1948 re-election victory over Thomas E. Dewey produced the iconic image of him holding up a Chicago Tribune declaring "Dewey Defeats Truman," an error rooted in the paper's early deadline and in Gallup's flawed landline-only polling (even the psychic horse "Lady Wonder" wrongly predicted a Dewey win); his campaign also financed the first-ever comic book biography of a presidential candidate. On November 1, 1950, Puerto Rican nationalists Oscar Collazo and Griselio Torresola, inspired by independence leader Pedro Albizu Campos, attempted to assassinate Truman outside Blair House; officer Leslie Coffelt was killed in the shootout, and Truman later joked that the attackers were "as stupid as they could be." Famously plain-spoken, Truman threatened to "beat the crap out of" critic Paul Hume for panning his daughter Margaret's concert, and the White House balcony bears his name. After leaving office he denounced Dwight Eisenhower for not repudiating Joseph McCarthy and endorsed Adlai Stevenson; the Former Presidents Act was passed largely on his behalf, and his namesakes include the University of Missouri's mascot.

links.text: Attempted assassination of Harry S. Truman -- Wikipedia

recursive_suggestions: Truman Capote -- shares the same search term ("Truman") and dominates the raw result set (16 bonus parts, 5 tossups); a wholly unrelated author, entirely excluded here.
Harper Lee and Truman Capote -- a paired literary answerline, excluded as unrelated to the president.
The 1952 steel strike -- surfaced as its own dense answerline distinct from Truman's seizure of the mills; excluded here, could support a standalone Labor History topic.

President Harry S. Truman's seizure of the steel mills -- also surfaced as its own independent answerline (Executive Order 10340, Youngstown Sheet & Tube Co. v. Sawyer, Justice Jackson's concurrence); only the brief mention of the episode from Truman's own answerline was kept here, but there is enough independent depth (tripartite framework, John W. Davis's arguments, Hugo Black's majority) for its own page.
Draft Eisenhower -- the 1948/1952 movement to nominate Eisenhower is its own answerline; excluded here though closely tied to Truman's decision not to seek re-election in 1952.

G. E. M. Anscombe -- surfaced independently via her essay "Mr. Truman's Degree" and broader philosophical work (consequentialism, Intention, translating Wittgenstein); only the Truman-specific fact was kept here.
Alben Barkley -- Truman's vice president, surfaced with independent biographical clue content (nicknamed "Iron Man," 1936 DNC keynote); not pursued here since the answerline was Barkley, not Truman.
Thomas Dewey -- Truman's 1948 opponent, surfaced independently (prosecuted Waxey Gordon, lost 1944 to FDR); only content where Truman himself was the answer was kept here.

"Do-Nothing" (80th) Congress -- surfaced as its own answerline including the Twenty-Second Amendment and the "Turnip Day Session"; only facts where Truman himself was the direct answer were kept in the Domestic Policy section here.
